iran_crude: Iran's crude exports held remarkably steady at ~2.0 mb/d through 9–29 Mar despite the Hormuz closure, as the IRGC's formalised 'toll booth' regime — vessels submit details to approved intermediaries, receive escort codes, tolls settled in yuan — effectively created a parallel transit system that keeps Kharg Island loading at near pre-war rates.

No destination breakdown is visible in tracking data, consistent with Iran's expanding dark fleet and STS transfer networks; Kpler reports 5 tankers loading ~7.7 mb on 29 Mar alone, suggesting throughput may accelerate further as the yuan-denominated toll system attracts opportunistic buyers.

The key risk is a US military operation to open the strait — 3,500 US troops arrived for potential Kharg raids per NBC News — which could disrupt the one Gulf exporter currently operating at near-normal capacity.
iran_naphtha: Iran's naphtha exports collapsed from 46 kbd (9–15 Mar, all to UAE) to zero from 16 Mar onward, likely reflecting both reduced refinery throughput from ~30% shut-in of southern oil fields and the breakdown of intra-Gulf trade as Hormuz blockade halted regional shipping.

The UAE was the sole buyer in W11 — a relationship that has now severed, removing a small but strategically significant feedstock supply for UAE petrochemical operations already stressed by Ruwais Refinery-2 shutdown.
iraq_crude: Iraq's crude exports collapsed from ~4.3 mb/d pre-war to effectively zero via Basra, then recovered to a steady 250 kbd via the restarted Kirkuk-Ceyhan pipeline to Turkey (first flows in over a decade under a Baghdad-KRG deal), covering only ~6% of lost southern capacity.

All tracked volumes now flow exclusively to Turkey — no seaborne exports visible since the FM declaration on all foreign-operated oilfields drove Basra output down to 900k bpd from 3.3M, with tanker nominations impossible per the Iraq Oil Ministry.

The pipeline ramp from ~30 kbd (9–15 Mar) to 250 kbd (23–29 Mar) shows the infrastructure is holding, but the 5 mb/d East-West capacity needed to meaningfully substitute for Basra does not exist — Iraq's export recovery is capped until Hormuz reopens.
iraq_naphtha: Iraq's naphtha exports dropped from a residual 15 kbd (9–15 Mar) to zero, consistent with the complete shutdown of Basra refineries and export terminals under the Iraq Oil Ministry FM declaration.
kuwait_crude: Kuwait's crude exports have effectively ceased — zero in 3 of 4 tracked weeks, with only a single 78 kbd blip (16–22 Mar) likely representing a final stored cargo clearing before full shutdown.

KPC's FM on crude exports from a 2.6 mb/d baseline, combined with the Al-Ahmadi refinery drone strikes (19–20 Mar, 730 kb/d offline), has eliminated Kuwait as a global crude supplier; KPC estimates 3–4 months to restore capacity even after hostilities end.
With no pipeline bypass and all terminals facing the Persian Gulf, Kuwait is the most structurally trapped of the Gulf exporters — storage is filling and further production cuts are inevitable.
kuwait_gasoil_diesel: Kuwait's diesel exports ceased entirely after 9–15 Mar, when a final 102 kbd of intra-Gulf shipments went to Qatar (48 kbd) and UAE (54 kbd) — likely emergency fuel supplies to regional neighbours before the blockade tightened.

The Al-Ahmadi refinery strikes and KPC FM have shut all refined product output; buyers in the Indian subcontinent and Asia that previously relied on Kuwaiti diesel are now competing for Saudi Yanbu and Omani cargoes.
kuwait_gasoline: Kuwait's gasoline exports zeroed out after a single 64 kbd shipment to Saudi Arabia (9–15 Mar) — an intra-Gulf cargo that predates the full Hormuz closure and Al-Ahmadi refinery destruction.

With Bapco (Bahrain) also offline and Iraqi refineries shut, the loss of Kuwait's refined product capacity has removed a combined ~1.5 mb/d of regional refining, intensifying the global products squeeze.
oman_crude: Oman's crude exports doubled from 550 kbd (9–15 Mar) to 1.1 mb/d (23–29 Mar), as the sultanate's non-Hormuz export position at Mina Al-Fahal makes it the only traditional Gulf exporter with unimpeded seaborne access — Oman crude is now trading at a ~$20/bbl premium to Brent (~$121/bbl) per Rigzone.

China is absorbing virtually all incremental volume (1.1 mb/d in 23–29 Mar vs. 550 kbd two weeks prior), with Taiwan briefly appearing as a buyer (269 kbd in 16–22 Mar), suggesting Asian refiners are paying premium to lock in whatever non-Russian, non-sanctioned barrels they can find.

Risk is concentrated on infrastructure: Salalah Port was struck again on 29 Mar (fuel tanks ablaze), and while inland production (~950 kb/d) remains intact, another hit on Mina Al-Fahal would remove the Gulf's last functioning crude export terminal.
oman_lng: Oman LNG's 10.4 Mtpa facility is now the last major Middle Eastern LNG plant running, exporting 30–43 ktons/d (9–29 Mar) — commanding significant Asian spot premiums with Qatar's entire 77 Mtpa offline since the Mar 18 missile strikes on Ras Laffan.

India has emerged as the dominant buyer from W12 onward (~21 ktons/d), replacing Japan and filling the gap left by Qatar's FM — consistent with Petronet LNG's scramble for alternative supply after declaring FM on its QatarEnergy contracts.

Customer rotation is notable: Thailand-Japan-Taiwan (W11) → India-China-Japan (W12) → India-Thailand (W13), reflecting a week-by-week bidding war for scarce LNG cargoes in a market that has lost 20% of global supply.
oman_kero_jet: Oman's jet fuel exports are sporadic and low-volume — a single 25 kbd cargo to Djibouti (16–22 Mar), likely bunkering supply for the US/French military base at Camp Lemonnier during heightened regional operations.
oman_gasoil_diesel: Oman's diesel exports doubled from 50 kbd (9–15 Mar, all Pakistan) to ~98 kbd (16–29 Mar), picking up stranded buyers from Kuwait and Saudi Gulf-side terminals — South Africa and Mauritius appeared as new customers, reflecting how far the supply disruption is reshaping trade routes.

Pakistan is the anchor buyer at ~50 kbd/d, consistent with its strategic proximity and bilateral transit arrangements with Iran for tanker passage; the widening customer base to Southern Africa signals Oman is now competing on trade routes previously dominated by Middle East Gulf exporters.
oman_gasoline: Oman's gasoline exports nearly tripled from 43 kbd (9–15 Mar) to 120 kbd (23–29 Mar), with Pakistan (73 kbd) and — notably — Saudi Arabia (47 kbd) as buyers in the latest week, suggesting Saudi domestic supply is tight enough to import gasoline despite running its own refineries.

The customer base rotated from East Africa (Tanzania, Sudan) to South Asia (Pakistan) and the Gulf (Saudi Arabia), as Oman's Sohar and Duqm refineries increasingly serve as the region's swing refined product supplier — a role that was unthinkable pre-crisis.
oman_naphtha: Oman's naphtha exports declined from 72 kbd (9–15 Mar, South Korea) to 29 kbd (16–22 Mar, India) before dropping to zero, likely reflecting a strategic decision to prioritize crude and refined products over petrochemical feedstock given the extreme supply-demand imbalance.
South Korea's naphtha export ban (enforced 27 Mar) may further tighten the Asian naphtha market, but Oman appears unable or unwilling to fill that gap given refinery capacity constraints.
qatar_crude: Qatar's crude exports remain effectively zero — all offshore oil fields (~440 kb/d) shut since early March — with only a residual 117 kbd blip in 16–22 Mar (crude to UAE, likely stored inventory clearing) before returning to zero.
With Laffan Refineries 1 and 2 offline and QatarEnergy's FM encompassing all hydrocarbon exports, Qatar has been completely removed from global crude markets for the first time in its modern history.
qatar_lng: Despite the declared total shutdown, Kpler tracking shows small LNG volumes still moving: 6.5 ktons/d to Kuwait (16–22 Mar) rising to 18.4 ktons/d split between Kuwait (9.7) and Pakistan (8.7) in 23–29 Mar — suggesting 1-2 undamaged trains may still be operational at reduced capacity.

These volumes are less than 10% of Qatar's pre-crisis ~200 ktons/d capacity, but critically important for Kuwait's power generation (Al-Zour FSRU) and Pakistan's grid; the Mar 18 missile strikes on Trains 4+6 (12.8 Mtpa, $20B/yr) face a 3–5 year repair timeline per QatarEnergy.

Edison has been notified of FM on 10 LNG cargoes through mid-June, and the FM extension to China, Italy, Belgium, and South Korea means Qatar's LNG supply gap will not close in 2026 regardless of conflict resolution.
saudi_arabia_crude: Saudi crude exports ramped from ~2.8 mb/d (9–15 Mar) to ~4.3 mb/d (23–29 Mar) via the East-West Pipeline to Yanbu, which hit its 7 mb/d full capacity per Bloomberg — with ~5 mb/d for exports and 2 mb/d feeding Red Sea refineries. This represents roughly half of Saudi's pre-war ~7 mb/d export volume.

China and India dominate the customer base — China at 1.1 mb/d and India at 1.0 mb/d in 23–29 Mar — but the mix is shifting: Japan and South Korea disappeared from mid-March loadings (likely drawing SPR instead), while Myanmar (572 kbd) and Thailand (285 kbd) emerged as significant new buyers, reflecting Southeast Asian refiners' desperation for non-Russian crude.

The Yanbu bottleneck is now the binding constraint on Saudi export recovery: at 7 mb/d pipeline throughput, crude exports are capped at ~5 mb/d and cannot grow further unless Hormuz reopens. Meanwhile, ~30 tankers in the Red Sea now sit within Houthi strike range, with the Bab al-Mandeb closure threat described as 'a viable option' per Houthi officials.
saudi_arabia_kero_jet: Saudi jet fuel exports doubled from 46 kbd (9–15 Mar) to 95 kbd (16–22 Mar) with the UK taking 46 kbd, before collapsing to zero in 23–29 Mar — the intermittent pattern suggests Yanbu refinery output is being prioritised for diesel and gasoline over aviation fuel.

The UK cargo likely reflects RAF logistical needs or commercial airline restocking via Red Sea routes; the inability to sustain jet fuel exports points to refinery capacity constraints even with the East-West Pipeline at maximum throughput.
saudi_arabia_gasoil_diesel: Saudi diesel exports via Yanbu are the most robust refined product flow in the Gulf, averaging 500–670 kbd across 9–29 Mar with a remarkably broad customer base spanning Egypt (anchor buyer at 150–220 kbd), Europe (Belgium, Italy, Spain, Slovenia), Southeast Asia (Malaysia, Singapore), and East Africa (Kenya, Sudan, South Africa).

Egypt's share has grown from 24% to 33% of Saudi diesel exports — its Red Sea proximity makes it the lowest-cost destination — while European buyers (Belgium at 106 kbd, Italy at 66 kbd) are paying premium to reroute from Gulf-sourced to Yanbu-sourced barrels, adding 5,000+ nautical miles to traditional supply chains.

The breadth of the customer base shows Saudi Arabia has effectively replaced multiple Gulf diesel exporters (Kuwait, Bahrain, UAE) as the sole swing supplier — but the Yanbu refinery constraint means global diesel markets remain structurally short.
saudi_arabia_gasoline: Saudi gasoline exports are declining — from 184 kbd (9–15 Mar) to 155 kbd (23–29 Mar) — with the customer base narrowing to a single buyer (Kenya at 45 kbd) in the latest week, suggesting Yanbu refinery throughput is being prioritized for crude and diesel over lower-margin gasoline.

The disappearance of Singapore, Oman, and Egypt from the gasoline buyer list, combined with Saudi Arabia itself importing gasoline from Oman (47 kbd in 23–29 Mar), signals that domestic consumption plus military fuel needs are absorbing an increasing share of refined output.
saudi_arabia_naphtha: Saudi naphtha exports are small and intermittent at 73–132 kbd, with Indonesia (42–73 kbd) as the primary buyer and Japan appearing briefly (28 kbd, 16–22 Mar) — volumes insufficient to offset the Asian naphtha crunch caused by the loss of Kuwait, Qatar, and UAE feedstock supply.

South Korea's YNCC has already declared FM on ethylene production due to total Middle East naphtha suspension, and Chandra Asri (Indonesia) has followed suit — the trickle of Saudi naphtha via Yanbu is a drop in the bucket for Asia's ~3 mb/d naphtha import demand.
uae_crude: UAE crude exports surged to 3.1 mb/d (23–29 Mar) from 1.2 mb/d the prior week — a dramatic recovery driven by ADNOC maximising Murban output through the Habshan-Fujairah pipeline, which bypasses Hormuz. ADNOC deferred all planned May-September maintenance to keep Fujairah loadings at capacity.

The customer base reshuffled dramatically: Thailand (786 kbd in W11) vanished entirely by W12, while Japan (500 kbd) and South Korea (543 kbd) returned in W13 after drawing SPR for two weeks. Vietnam (257 kbd) and Philippines (143 kbd) appeared as new buyers — Southeast Asian refiners that had never taken Murban crude before are now competing for allocations.

ADNOC issued its first post-war Murban availability forecast on 27 Mar, signalling confidence in sustained Fujairah exports — but the IRGC strike on Al Taweelah aluminium (29 Mar, 6 injured) is a reminder that UAE infrastructure outside Hormuz is not immune to Iranian retaliation.
uae_gasoil_diesel: UAE diesel exports ceased entirely after 9–15 Mar (73 kbd to Pakistan), consistent with Ruwais Refinery-2 going offline and the Shah Gas Field remaining shut (~1 Bcf/d) — removing a major NGL and diesel source from global supply.

The complete halt in UAE refined product exports stands in stark contrast to the crude recovery at Fujairah, highlighting that bypass pipeline capacity exists for crude but not for processed products — refineries inside the Gulf (Ruwais, Jebel Ali) cannot ship through Hormuz.
uae_naphtha: UAE naphtha exports were zero for three weeks before a single 46 kbd cargo to Taiwan appeared (23–29 Mar), likely sourced from Fujairah condensate processing rather than the shuttered Ruwais complex — a one-off rather than a sustainable flow.

Taiwan's scramble for any available naphtha cargo — after South Korea banned naphtha exports on 27 Mar — underscores the severity of the Asian petrochemical feedstock crisis driven by the simultaneous loss of Saudi, Kuwait, Qatar, and UAE NGL-linked naphtha.
russia_crude: Russian crude exports declined 20% from ~6.7 mb/d (9–15 Mar) to ~5.4 mb/d (23–29 Mar), even as the US 30-day sanctions waiver allowed buyers to accept cargoes loaded before 12 Mar — suggesting the waiver-eligible window is now closing and underlying demand is normalizing.

China remains the top buyer but fell sharply from 2.4 mb/d to 1.3 mb/d over the period; India similarly contracted from 1.4 to 0.9 mb/d. Both are likely reaching storage and refinery-intake limits after aggressive Jan–Feb stockpiling (China imported 12.0 mb/d in Jan–Feb, +16% YoY per S&P Global).

Turkey and Italy surged as new swing buyers — Turkey hit 705 kbd (23–29 Mar, 5x its 9–15 Mar level), Italy reached 829 kbd — consistent with European refiners scrambling for non-sanctioned medium-sour barrels to replace lost Gulf supply. With CREA reporting 56% of Russian crude now carried by shadow-fleet tankers, enforcement risk remains elevated.

The ESPO pipeline (~600 kbd to China) provides a sanctions-insulated floor, but seaborne volumes face a cliff when the EU's proposed comprehensive services ban takes effect — watch for further customer-mix rotation toward Turkey and India.
russia_lng: Russian LNG exports held relatively steady at 81–111 ktons/d across March, with EU destinations (France, Spain, Belgium, Netherlands) absorbing the majority — ironically, Russia routed 100% of February Yamal LNG exports to the EU per CREA, even as the EU prepares to ban Russian LNG purchases effective 25 Apr.

The imminent EU ban will force ~60–70% of Russian LNG volumes to find new buyers. China appeared intermittently (28 ktons/d in 16–22 Mar, absent in 23–29 Mar), while Japan took 18–19 ktons/d when available — neither sufficient to absorb the redirected EU volumes, especially with Arctic LNG-2 still operating below capacity.
Post-25 Apr, expect Russian LNG to trade at deep discounts to attract price-sensitive Asian buyers, compounding the global spot tightness already driven by Qatar's 77 Mtpa being offline.
russia_gasoil_diesel: Russian diesel exports collapsed 74% from 932 kbd (9–15 Mar) to 241 kbd (23–29 Mar), with Turkey — the dominant buyer — halving from 411 to 123 kbd. Brazil, Egypt, and Ghana all disappeared from the customer list by late March.

The contraction likely reflects a combination of Russia's seasonal domestic fuel-supply restrictions, refinery maintenance, and growing buyer hesitancy ahead of potential tighter EU sanctions on Russian product trade services. Turkey's declining volumes suggest Ankara is diversifying toward US Gulf Coast diesel, which surged 27% over the same period.
russia_gasoline: Russian gasoline exports were negligible — 41 kbd to Libya in 9–15 Mar, then zero for the remainder of March. Russia's domestic gasoline export ban (in effect since 2024) continues to lock these volumes inside the country, making Russia irrelevant as a gasoline supplier despite the global shortage triggered by Gulf refinery shutdowns (Bahrain's Sitra 405 kbd, Kuwait's Al-Ahmadi 730 kbd both offline).
russia_naphtha: Russian naphtha exports fell 55% from 677 kbd (9–15 Mar) to 307 kbd (23–29 Mar), with China dropping from 231 kbd to zero and India halving from 155 to 51 kbd — suggesting Asian petrochemical crackers are hitting feedstock-processing limits rather than supply constraints.

Morocco and Brazil emerged as mid-month buyers (98 and 87 kbd respectively in 16–22 Mar) before fading, indicating opportunistic spot purchases rather than structural demand. South Korea's 27 Mar naphtha export ban (per Yonhap) signals that Asian producers are hoarding feedstock domestically.
us_crude: US crude exports rose 13% from ~3.8 mb/d (9–15 Mar) to ~4.3 mb/d (23–29 Mar), with the customer base broadening dramatically as Gulf barrels disappeared — China (224 kbd), Japan (286 kbd), and India (249 kbd) all appeared as buyers in 23–29 Mar, having been absent or minimal in the first week.

South Korea remained the anchor buyer at 507–807 kbd across March, while the Netherlands served as the primary European transit hub at 600–700 kbd. The 45.2 mb SPR exchange already delivered (of 172 mb committed) is freeing domestic barrels for export.

At ~4.3 mb/d, US crude exports are approaching the practical ceiling set by Gulf Coast terminal capacity and Permian takeaway constraints — further growth would require either WTI discounts to incentivize pipeline-to-port optimization or new export infrastructure.
us_lng: US LNG exports flatlined at ~370 ktons/d across all three weeks of March, confirming that all 8 US terminals are running at maximum nameplate capacity (~14.6 Bcf/d per EIA). The US is now the world's largest LNG exporter with Qatar's 77 Mtpa offline, but physically cannot grow volumes without new terminals.

Destination diversification is striking: 13–20 countries per week, with traditional European buyers (Netherlands, Spain, France, Germany) sharing cargoes with crisis-driven Asian entrants like Thailand (34 ktons/d in 23–29 Mar), Bangladesh (10 ktons/d), and India (20 ktons/d in 16–22 Mar).

The ~20% global LNG supply gap from Qatar cannot be closed by US capacity alone — next meaningful additions (Plaquemines Phase 2, Golden Pass) won't deliver until late 2027, leaving spot prices structurally elevated at $25+/MMBtu through at least mid-2026.
us_lpg: US LPG exports rose 12% to 165 ktons/d (23–29 Mar) as Southeast Asian buyers rushed in to replace lost Gulf NGL supply — Indonesia surged from 1.8 to 13.3 ktons/d and Vietnam held at 6.3 ktons/d, both previously minimal US LPG customers.

Japan remained the largest single buyer but its take halved from 33 to 16 ktons/d, likely as Japanese importers diversified across more sources. Enterprise and Targa Gulf Coast terminals are benefiting from the sharply favorable LPG-naphtha spread (per Argus), which incentivizes higher US NGL exports into the PDH plant feedstock market.
us_kero_jet: US jet fuel exports were volatile at 214–338 kbd across March, with the UK emerging as the dominant buyer at 84–87 kbd in the latter two weeks — likely reflecting the loss of Middle East jet supply that previously transited through Fujairah's bunkering hub (now under FM from MEE).

LATAM remained the steady base (Mexico 39 kbd, Honduras 9–15 kbd, Costa Rica 22 kbd), while European destinations (Netherlands 43 kbd, Canada 40–52 kbd) appeared in 23–29 Mar as Atlantic Basin jet fuel tightened.
us_gasoil_diesel: US diesel exports surged 27% from ~1.2 mb/d (9–15 Mar) to ~1.5 mb/d (23–29 Mar), the strongest rate in March, with the Netherlands becoming the dominant destination at 385 kbd — up from zero in the first week — consistent with ARA hub restocking as EU refiners cut throughput on lost Gulf crude supply.

Australia appeared as a new buyer at 87 kbd (23–29 Mar), confirming Bloomberg reporting that Australia is leveraging its LNG exports to secure fuel supplies in return, as the country grapples with shortages from the Hormuz closure. Record US fuel shipments to Australia in March 2026 represent a structural shift in Pacific Basin diesel trade.
The surge to 1.5 mb/d approaches US Gulf Coast diesel export capacity limits; sustained demand at this level would pressure domestic inventories and potentially trigger DOE monitoring.
us_gasoline: US gasoline exports held steady at 760–858 kbd across March, with Mexico dominating at 195–311 kbd — the narrowing range reflecting Mexico's own refinery challenges (Dos Bocas still ramping, legacy plants underperforming). Colombia, Honduras, and Ecuador provided the LATAM floor.

Australia appeared as a buyer in 16–22 Mar (64 kbd), its first significant US gasoline purchase — per IndexBox, record US fuel shipments to Australia in March reflect Canberra leveraging energy-export diplomacy to secure refined product amid the Iran-war fuel crisis.
us_naphtha: US naphtha exports spiked to 738 kbd in 16–22 Mar — nearly double the adjacent weeks — driven entirely by South Korea surging to 203 kbd as Korean crackers (YNCC, others) scrambled for feedstock after Middle East naphtha supply was cut off by the Hormuz closure and YNCC's FM declaration.

South Korea's absence from 23–29 Mar (after Seoul imposed a naphtha export ban on 27 Mar per Yonhap) suggests Korean importers frontloaded purchases before the ban locked remaining supply domestically. Venezuela (66–72 kbd) and Japan (32–126 kbd) remain consistent baseline customers.
australia_crude: Australian crude exports declined 35% from 280 kbd (9–15 Mar) to 183 kbd (23–29 Mar), with the customer base narrowing to South Korea only (106 kbd) by late March — reflecting Australia's limited exportable surplus as a net crude importer.

The concentration on South Korea is notable: Seoul appears to be securing every non-Gulf barrel available in the Pacific Basin, consistent with its 208-day strategic reserve drawdown strategy. Australia's inland crude production (~350 kbd) is largely committed to domestic refineries, capping export upside.
australia_lng: Australian LNG exports fell 33% from 252 ktons/d (9–15 Mar) to 169 ktons/d (23–29 Mar), with the late-March decline directly attributable to Tropical Cyclone Narelle knocking NW Shelf, one Gorgon train, and a Wheatstone platform offline per Bloomberg — removing ~8% of global LNG supply on top of Qatar's 77 Mtpa already offline.

Japan remained the anchor buyer at 48–71 ktons/d, while India appeared for the first time at 8 ktons/d (23–29 Mar), signaling New Delhi's desperate diversification after Petronet LNG declared FM on Qatar contracts. Singapore dropped out entirely in 23–29 Mar.

The cyclone timing is devastating: with Qatar offline and US terminals maxed out, Australia was one of the last swing LNG suppliers available to Asia. Oman LNG (10.4 Mtpa) and Papua New Guinea are now the only meaningful non-disrupted LNG sources for the region.
australia_naphtha: Australian naphtha exports were sporadic and minimal — 49 kbd to Singapore (9–15 Mar) and 49 kbd to Malaysia (23–29 Mar), with nothing in between. Australia is not a significant naphtha exporter; these small volumes likely represent condensate-adjacent cargoes from NW Shelf gas processing.
eu_27_crude: EU-27 crude exports were minimal and volatile at 46–198 kbd across March, entirely intra-European (UK, Finland, Netherlands, Turkey) with zero Gulf exposure. These flows represent North Sea and Mediterranean crude redistribution rather than any meaningful export activity.
The collapse to 46 kbd in 16–22 Mar (Turkey only) and partial recovery to 144 kbd in 23–29 Mar reflects EU refiners hoarding crude domestically as Gulf supply channels remain severed.
eu_27_kero_jet: EU-27 jet fuel exports collapsed from 146 kbd (9–15 Mar) to just 27 kbd (23–29 Mar), an 82% decline — consistent with European refiners slashing throughput as Gulf crude feedstock dries up and airlines compete for tightening domestic jet supply.

France, the largest destination in 9–15 Mar (51 kbd), disappeared entirely by 23–29 Mar, while only Norway (17 kbd) and UK (10 kbd) received cargoes — suggesting intra-EU jet supply is being rationed to the most critical routes.
eu_27_gasoil_diesel: EU-27 diesel exports collapsed 61% from ~1.1 mb/d (9–15 Mar) to 429 kbd (23–29 Mar), as European refiners — starved of Gulf crude feedstock — cut throughput and redirected product to domestic markets. Poland's take fell from 245 to 49 kbd; Germany nearly vanished (67 to 1 kbd).

The remaining 23–29 Mar exports shifted to Balkans/Adriatic destinations (Croatia 96 kbd, Slovenia 51 kbd), likely reflecting pipeline-connected redistribution from Med refineries rather than discretionary exports. This contraction is the flip side of surging US diesel exports to the ARA hub.
eu_27_gasoline: EU-27 gasoline exports remained robust at 1.1–1.3 mb/d across March, but the destination mix reshuffled dramatically: Saudi Arabia received 75 kbd in 9–15 Mar (6.2% Gulf share) before disappearing entirely as Hormuz transit became impossible — one of the few direct Gulf-share observations in this batch.

Africa emerged as the growth market: Kenya (111 kbd), Ghana (99 kbd), Nigeria (92 kbd), and Senegal (49 kbd) all appeared as significant buyers in late March, consistent with West African countries diversifying away from disrupted Gulf product supplies. Egypt remained a steady buyer at 78–111 kbd throughout.

EU gasoline surplus is sustained by lower domestic driving demand and continued Russian crude-via-India product flows — but if EU refiners further cut throughput on feedstock shortages, this export volume is at risk.
eu_27_naphtha: EU-27 naphtha exports declined 35% from 413 kbd (9–15 Mar) to 267 kbd (23–29 Mar), but the Asia-bound share increased — Japan appeared at 16–32 kbd, China held at 49–73 kbd — signaling desperate Asian petrochemical crackers sourcing feedstock from any available origin after Gulf naphtha supply was cut off.

The decline reflects tightening EU refinery output (less naphtha as a byproduct of reduced crude throughput) rather than demand weakness. Spain's outsized 180 kbd take in 16–22 Mar was likely an intra-EU redistribution for Repsol's crackers, not an export in the traditional sense.
china_crude: China crude imports plunged 29% to ~7.9 mb/d (23–29 Mar) from 11.2 mb/d two weeks prior, as Saudi Arabia and UAE — which together supplied 2.3 mb/d in early March — effectively disappeared from the supplier mix after Hormuz tightened further. Russia remains the anchor at 2.1 mb/d (~27% share) but is near ESPO pipeline capacity limits.

Oman surged to 1.5 mb/d (23–29 Mar), triple its early-March rate, capitalising on its non-Hormuz export position at Mina Al-Fahal — though the Mar 29 Salalah port strike (fuel tanks ablaze per Al Jazeera) adds risk. Iraq collapsed from 781 kbd to just 73 kbd, consistent with the Mar 20 FM on all foreign-operated oilfields.

Brazil (1.1 mb/d) and Iran (602 kbd via IRGC-tolled passage in yuan) provide remaining diversification, but the 3.3 mb/d gap from lost Gulf barrels far exceeds alternatives. China’s Jan–Feb stockpiling (+16% YoY per Bloomberg) is now being drawn at an unsustainable rate — commercial reserves likely fall below 30-day cover by mid-April without recovery.
china_lng: Qatar LNG to China dropped from 45 ktons/d (9–15 Mar) to zero by 23–29 Mar — a complete shutoff following QatarEnergy’s FM extension to Chinese contracts on Mar 24. Total LNG imports fell 39% to 82 ktons/d as the ~20% global LNG supply gap (per Wood Mackenzie) leaves few spot alternatives.

Australia has become China’s de facto LNG lifeline at 37 ktons/d (46% share, 23–29 Mar), with Malaysia (15 ktons/d), PNG, and Indonesia filling gaps. Russian Yamal LNG dropped from 24 to 9 ktons/d — likely diverting cargoes to higher-priced European spot ahead of the EU’s Apr 25 ban.

With Asian spot LNG prices up ~39% and Power of Siberia pipeline providing only partial insulation, China faces a structural LNG deficit through Q2 unless Hormuz reopens or US export terminals (already at 110% nameplate utilisation per Fortune) can redirect volume from Europe.
china_lpg: US Gulf Coast LPG surged to 31.8 ktons/d (23–29 Mar), now supplying half of China’s imports, as Saudi, Kuwait, and Qatar NGL-linked supply collapsed alongside crude production shutdowns. Canada (13.2 ktons/d) has tripled its pre-crisis share.

Iran remains the only Gulf LPG supplier at 14.9 ktons/d via IRGC-tolled passage, but PDH plant operators are scrambling for alternatives at sharply higher prices per ICIS. The LPG-naphtha spread now favours increased US exports, likely sustaining this trans-Pacific flow through Q2.
china_kero_jet: Jet fuel imports collapsed to zero after 9–15 Mar (24 kbd from South Korea only) as China’s Mar 4 refined fuel export ban and regional refinery throughput cuts (Sinopec, Zhejiang Petrochemical trimming 10%+) eliminated surplus product availability across Asia.
china_gasoil_diesel: Diesel imports remain a minor intra-Asian flow at ~12 kbd (23–29 Mar), entirely from South Korea — reflecting China’s massive domestic refining capacity rather than any Gulf supply dependency. China’s refined fuel export ban has effectively walled off the domestic market.
china_naphtha: Gulf naphtha suppliers (Kuwait, UAE, Bahrain, Qatar) all disappeared by 23–29 Mar — consistent with FM declarations from KPC, Bapco, and QatarEnergy. Russia surged to 106 kbd (38% share), with Norway (77 kbd) and South Korea (49 kbd) emerging as non-traditional replacement suppliers.

Wanhua Chemical’s FM on Middle East feedstock (2.2 Mtpa ethylene capacity at Yantai at risk) underscores the petrochemical chain reaction from Gulf naphtha loss. Total naphtha imports dropped 32% from early-March levels, signalling cracker utilisation cuts ahead.
india_crude: India’s crude imports stabilised at ~4.3 mb/d (23–29 Mar) after the sharp early-March contraction, with Gulf share recovering to 34.5% as Saudi (951 kbd via Yanbu pipeline) and UAE (486 kbd via Fujairah) resume shipments outside Hormuz. Operation Urja Suraksha naval escorts and Iran’s ‘friendly nation’ safe-passage status are enabling flows that other Asian importers cannot access.

Russia remains the dominant supplier at 1.65 mb/d (38% share), nearly double pre-crisis levels — India-Russia crude talks restarted Mar 19 per Business Today. Nigeria (413 kbd), Ecuador (302 kbd), and Colombia (271 kbd) have entered the mix as refiners compete for every available non-Gulf barrel.

The trade deficit is projected to widen >$4B in March alone. MRPL’s FM on gasoline exports (500 kbd throughput cuts) signals that even with recovering crude, domestic fuel production remains constrained — India is importing crude but losing refined product output.
india_lng: Qatar has completely disappeared from India’s LNG supply despite being 41.4% of pre-crisis imports — consistent with Petronet LNG’s FM declaration (7.5 Mtpa Dahej terminal impacted). Gujarat Gas’s 50% industrial supply cut remains in force, signalling demand destruction is absorbing the gap.

Oman’s non-Hormuz LNG facility became India’s primary Gulf source at 32 ktons/d (23–29 Mar), with US (19 ktons/d) and Nigerian cargoes (18 ktons/d) filling the rest. Total imports recovered to 79 ktons/d from a 31 ktons/d trough — but still well below India’s pre-crisis ~120 ktons/d run rate.
india_lpg: Indian Navy-escorted LPG convoys (Pine Gas and Jag Vasant, 92,000t combined per Business Today) arrived 26–28 Mar, boosting imports to 18.1 ktons/d — the highest level this month. US supply surged to 10.8 ktons/d (60% share) as Enterprise and Targa terminals ramp Gulf Coast loadings.

Gulf LPG (Saudi, Kuwait, Qatar, UAE) still trickling through at 7.3 ktons/d via military convoy, but the model is bandwidth-limited. With millions of households affected by LPG shortages per Al Jazeera, this remains a critical humanitarian supply chain.
india_naphtha: Russia dominates India’s naphtha supply at 129 kbd (74% share, 23–29 Mar), up from 53 kbd in early March — the fastest-growing bilateral petrochemical feedstock flow in the crisis. Oman (46 kbd) is the only Gulf supplier, using its non-Hormuz position.

UAE naphtha (48 kbd in early March) completely disappeared after Ruwais Refinery-2 went offline and Shah Gas Field shut (~1 Bcf/d). Total imports surged to 175 kbd (23–29 Mar), suggesting Indian crackers are rebuilding feedstock inventories drawn down in mid-March.
japan_crude: Japan’s crude imports collapsed 47% to ~1.1 mb/d (23–29 Mar) from 2.0 mb/d the prior week — the most dramatic single-week decline among major Asian importers. Gulf share remains 95.5% with zero non-Gulf diversification, leaving Japan uniquely exposed as the only major importer without a Russia or Atlantic Basin backup.

METI released 80 mb from strategic reserves (largest-ever, oil flowing from 10 national stockpile sites since Mar 16 per Japan Times), buying time but not solving the 93.5% ME crude dependency. Saudi (611 kbd) and UAE (395 kbd) are shipping via pipeline bypass routes at half pre-crisis volumes and declining.

Japan is ‘considering’ Russian crude purchases (per Moscow Times) — a potential geopolitical watershed for a G7 sanctions coalition member. Without either Russian supply or Hormuz reopening, Japan’s SPR drawdown trajectory hits critical levels by late April.
japan_lng: Australia has become Japan’s overwhelming LNG anchor at 111 ktons/d (67% share, 23–29 Mar), up from 81 ktons/d in early March, as Qatar dropped to zero post-FM. Total imports declined 17% to 167 ktons/d, but Japan’s diversified contract portfolio (Malaysia, PNG, Indonesia, Russia, Canada) provides notably more resilience than crude.

Daiwa Institute’s warning that a 10% ME LNG cut would push Japan’s GDP negative remains the key macro risk. Asian LNG spot now outpaces European gains by $1–3/MMBtu per Euronews, diverting flexible cargoes eastward — a temporary tailwind but unsustainable at current price levels.
japan_lpg: Japan’s LPG imports are now 100% US-sourced at 34.9 ktons/d (23–29 Mar), a complete structural shift from the pre-crisis mix where Saudi Arabia was historically a major supplier. US Gulf Coast terminals have fully absorbed the displaced Saudi/Kuwait NGL volumes.
Volumes are holding steady (up from 17.6 ktons/d in 16–22 Mar), suggesting US export capacity can sustain Japan’s LPG needs — but at significantly higher trans-Pacific freight costs.
japan_kero_jet: Jet fuel imports are entirely from South Korea at 70 kbd (23–29 Mar), stable since mid-March. This intra-Asian refinery product swap reflects no Gulf dependency in this product — South Korean refiners are redirecting jet fuel to Japan as their own crude imports contract.
japan_gasoil_diesel: Small diesel volumes (~16 kbd) sourced almost entirely from South Korea, with a one-off 50 kbd Brunei cargo in 16–22 Mar. No Gulf dependency, but volumes signal Japan’s domestic refinery utilisation is tightening as crude supply contracts.
japan_gasoline: Gasoline imports from South Korea tripled to 85 kbd (23–29 Mar) from 27 kbd in early March — a direct signal that Japanese refinery throughput cuts (from crude shortages) are forcing increased reliance on Korean product imports to meet domestic demand.
South Korea’s own naphtha export ban (Mar 27) has not yet constrained gasoline flows to Japan, but the risk of Korean product rationing rises as Seoul prioritises domestic supply.
japan_naphtha: Kuwait disappeared from Japan’s naphtha supply (was 70 kbd in early March) after KPC’s FM declaration and Al-Ahmadi refinery drone strikes. UAE surged to 170 kbd (76% share, 23–29 Mar) using Fujairah loadings — the highest single-supplier naphtha concentration Japan has experienced.

Total naphtha imports recovered to 225 kbd from an 86 kbd trough, but five different supplier mixes in four weeks signals extreme fragility in Japan’s petrochemical feedstock chain. YNCC-style cracker shutdowns could spread to Japanese operators if UAE supply falters.
south_korea_crude: South Korea’s crude imports collapsed 56% to ~1.4 mb/d (23–29 Mar) from 3.2 mb/d in early March — the steepest proportional decline of any major Asian importer. The 208-day SPR buffer (22.5 mb record release authorised per S&P Global, 6.0 mb released by Mar 28) is the critical backstop.

UAE (589 kbd) overtook Saudi Arabia (218 kbd) as top supplier in 23–29 Mar, likely via Fujairah bypass. US crude surged to 150 kbd with Algeria (154 kbd), Australia (81 kbd), and Canada (83 kbd) appearing for the first time — the most aggressive real-time supplier diversification of any Asian importer.
The Mar 27 naphtha export ban signals Seoul is reserving available crude for domestic petrochemical production over export revenue — a defensive posture that compounds Japan’s naphtha supply risk.
south_korea_lng: Qatar has fully disappeared from South Korea’s LNG supply (was 16.7 ktons/d in early March), consistent with QatarEnergy’s Mar 24 FM extension to Korean contracts. KOGAS reports inventory exceeds mandatory reserves, buffered by the new Woodside 0.5 Mtpa deal (commenced 2026) and existing Australian contracts.

Total imports fell 50% to 101 ktons/d but the supply mix is remarkably diversified: Australia (48 ktons/d), US (18 ktons/d), Indonesia (17 ktons/d), Malaysia (10 ktons/d), and even re-exported Chinese LNG (8 ktons/d). South Korea’s pre-crisis portfolio management is paying off relative to Japan’s concentrated crude dependency.
south_korea_lpg: LPG imports are 100% US + Canada sourced at 10 ktons/d (23–29 Mar), with zero Gulf supply — mirroring Japan’s complete structural shift to North American NGL. Declining volumes (from 18 ktons/d in early March) likely reflect slowing PDH and petrochemical demand following YNCC’s ethylene FM.
south_korea_gasoil_diesel: Russian diesel appeared in South Korea’s supply mix for the first time at 7.5 kbd (23–29 Mar), replacing traditional Japanese supply — a notable geopolitical realignment in the product market as Seoul pragmatically sources fuel regardless of origin.
south_korea_gasoline: Gasoline imports collapsed to zero in 23–29 Mar from 108 kbd (Japan-sourced) in early March, likely reflecting both reduced mobility demand from economic slowdown and domestic refinery output being prioritised for internal consumption over product trade.
south_korea_naphtha: Gulf naphtha share cratered from 96% (9–15 Mar) to 30% (23–29 Mar) — the most dramatic feedstock supply shift in the crisis. UAE, Qatar, Bahrain, and Saudi virtually all disappeared, replaced by a patchwork of Oman (70 kbd), Greece (62 kbd), Algeria (53 kbd), Peru (49 kbd), and Egypt (47 kbd).

YNCC’s FM on ethylene production (total naphtha cracker feedstock suspension) is the downstream consequence. Total imports fell 30% to 344 kbd, and the new Oman-Greece-Algeria supply chain is both more expensive and less reliable — Korean petrochemical margins face sustained compression.
thailand_crude: Thailand's crude imports fell 39% from ~1.28 mb/d (9–15 Mar) to ~0.79 mb/d (23–29 Mar), but Gulf share paradoxically surged from 31% to 94% as non-Gulf alternatives (US, Brazil, Libya, Australia) dried up — leaving Thailand almost entirely dependent on UAE Murban cargoes routed outside Hormuz via Fujairah.

Supplier diversity collapsed from 7 countries to just UAE + Malaysia by 23–29 Mar, a dangerous concentration for a country where oil imports equal 4.7% of GDP (highest in ASEAN). Bangkok’s bilateral ‘friendly nation’ transit deal with Iran on Mar 28 may restore access to broader Gulf barrels, following a Bangchak tanker’s safe Hormuz passage on Mar 23.

At current drawdown trajectory (~0.5 mb/d below pre-crisis norms), Thailand’s reserves provide a limited buffer; the real risk is summer electricity demand if Hormuz remains contested and UAE supply alone cannot scale.
thailand_lng: Qatar — which supplied a significant share of Thailand’s LNG pre-crisis — is completely absent across all of March, consistent with QatarEnergy’s blanket FM on 77 Mtpa of capacity. Thailand has successfully diversified to Australia, Mozambique, Canada, and Malaysia, holding volumes at 25–43 ktons/d.

Oman LNG emerged as a new Gulf-adjacent supplier (10 ktons/d in 23–29 Mar), leveraging its non-Hormuz export position. A US cargo appeared on Mar 30, signalling Cheniere spot cargoes are reaching Southeast Asian buyers as US terminals run at 110% nameplate utilisation.
thailand_gasoil_diesel: Diesel imports remain minimal (8–46 kbd) and entirely from regional suppliers — Malaysia and India — reflecting Thailand’s largely self-sufficient domestic refining capacity. India’s appearance as a diesel supplier (46 kbd in 16–22 Mar) signals Indian refiners redirecting product to nearby markets as Gulf crude constraints alter traditional flow patterns.
thailand_gasoline: Gasoline imports nearly tripled from ~5 kbd (9–15 Mar) to ~16 kbd (23–29 Mar), sourced from Singapore and Malaysia. The rising trajectory suggests domestic refining throughput is under pressure from crude supply constraints, forcing increased product imports from ASEAN trading hubs.
thailand_naphtha: Critical feedstock disruption: Bahrain (sole supplier at 37 kbd in 9–15 Mar) vanished entirely after IRGC missile strikes destroyed Bapco’s Sitra refinery (405 kb/d offline), leaving Thailand with zero naphtha supply for all of 16–22 Mar.

Singapore sent a token 3 kbd replacement in 23–29 Mar — barely 9% of pre-disruption volumes — far below Thailand’s petrochemical feedstock needs. Thai naphtha crackers face potential shutdowns if alternative supply cannot be secured from South Korea or India.
vietnam_crude: Catastrophic supply failure: Kuwait (sole supplier at 286 kbd in 9–15 Mar) was cut off entirely following KPC’s FM declaration, collapsing Vietnam’s crude imports to zero in 23–29 Mar — the first complete crude import blackout for Vietnam’s Nghi Son refinery.

Emergency diversification produced only sporadic relief — a Nigerian cargo (88 kbd in 16–22 Mar) and a Libyan cargo (175 kbd on Mar 30). Hanoi’s Decree 72/2026 slashed MFN tariffs on crude to 0% and PM Chính called Kuwait, Qatar, Algeria, and Japan to plead for supply. Vietnam reportedly secured 4 million additional barrels from international partners, but this covers barely 2 weeks of refinery demand.
With 85% of crude historically from Kuwait alone, Vietnam’s extreme single-source concentration is the most dangerous supply chain vulnerability among Asian importers.
vietnam_kero_jet: Jet fuel imports declined from 20 kbd (9–15 Mar) to 13 kbd (23–29 Mar), shifting from China to Singapore as sole supplier after Beijing’s Mar 4–5 refined fuel export ban removed Chinese supply. Volumes remain sufficient for Vietnam’s aviation sector but at sharply higher spot prices.
vietnam_gasoil_diesel: Diesel imports surged nearly 3x from 77 kbd (9–15 Mar) to 227 kbd (23–29 Mar), with South Korea as the dominant supplier at ~167 kbd — a direct consequence of Nghi Son refinery’s loss of Kuwaiti crude feedstock forcing Vietnam to import finished product instead.

Taiwan appeared as a new diesel supplier (48 kbd in 23–29 Mar), likely re-exporting from its own tightening refinery production. The crude-to-product substitution compounds Vietnam’s trade deficit: importing refined diesel at $120+/bbl equivalent costs significantly more than processing $80–90/bbl Kuwait crude domestically.
vietnam_gasoline: Gasoline imports halved from 43 kbd (9–15 Mar) to 23 kbd (23–29 Mar), with Singapore as sole remaining supplier after Japan disappeared — likely conserving domestic supply given its own 93.5% ME crude dependence. The 0% MFN tariff under Decree 72/2026 is easing procurement costs but cannot resolve physical supply tightness across Asia.
vietnam_naphtha: Effectively zero naphtha imports throughout March, consistent with Vietnam’s limited naphtha-cracking petrochemical capacity. A single Russian cargo appeared on Mar 30 (329 kbd for 1 day), potentially signalling the start of Russia-Vietnam energy cooperation following the bilateral deal signed Mar 23.
united_states_crude: US seaborne crude imports held at ~2.5–3.1 mb/d across March, demonstrating the resilience of hemispheric diversification: Venezuela (~390–490 kbd), Mexico (~250–560 kbd), Canada (~170–480 kbd), Brazil (~135–294 kbd), Guyana (~143–198 kbd), Colombia, Argentina, and Ecuador all actively supplying.

Gulf crude (Saudi via Yanbu + Iraq via restarted Kirkuk-Ceyhan) still flowing at 0.5–0.7 mb/d, but Gulf share fluctuated between 18–26%. Saudi Arabia remained the single largest supplier in 2 of 3 full weeks, suggesting Yanbu pipeline bypass cargoes are reaching US Gulf Coast refineries.

The IEA’s historic 400 mb coordinated release (largest ever) and 45.2 mb of US SPR already delivered are providing a ceiling on price spikes, but Goldman’s $135/bbl scenario remains a risk if Bab al-Mandeb also closes.
united_states_kero_jet: South Korea dominates US jet fuel imports at 60–137 kbd, but volumes halved from 204 kbd (9–15 Mar) to ~100 kbd (23–29 Mar) as Asian refiners tighten export allocations. China dropped out entirely after Beijing’s fuel export ban, removing ~44 kbd of supply.
Taiwan appeared as a small new source (12 kbd in 23–29 Mar), likely from CPC or Formosa Plastics refineries seeking to monetise surplus product as domestic crude throughput falls.
united_states_gasoil_diesel: US diesel imports are fully hemispheric with zero Gulf exposure — Canada supplies 89–178 kbd (85–95% share), supplemented by Mexico, Argentina, and South Korea. The North American pipeline and rail network provides a natural buffer that most Asian importers lack.
Turkey appeared as a diesel supplier in 9–15 Mar (64 kbd) but dropped out by W12, possibly as Turkish refiners redirected product to higher-premium European buyers.
united_states_gasoline: Gasoline imports are diversified across Atlantic/Caribbean sources — Canada (97–133 kbd), Bahamas (53–107 kbd), UK (12–58 kbd), and USVI (42–43 kbd) — with zero Gulf share throughout March. India appeared as a new supplier at ~44–49 kbd, consistent with MRPL redirecting gasoline exports westward after declaring FM on Gulf-crude dependent production.

India’s and South Korea’s emergence as US gasoline suppliers (combined ~70 kbd in 23–29 Mar) reflects the global reshuffling of refined product flows as traditional Gulf-supplied Asian refiners seek new export outlets.
united_states_naphtha: Naphtha sourcing shifted entirely from European suppliers (Belgium, Netherlands, France at 67 kbd in 9–15 Mar) to South American and African origins (Angola, Brazil, Argentina at 96 kbd in 23–29 Mar). European naphtha is likely being retained domestically as EU refiners face their own crude supply tightening from the Gulf disruption.
The source rotation also reflects strengthening Atlantic Basin trade corridors as global flows reorganise around the Hormuz closure.
taiwan_crude: Taiwan’s crude imports collapsed 66% from ~864 kbd (9–15 Mar) to ~294 kbd (23–29 Mar), with Gulf share plunging from 66% to zero. Kuwait vanished after W11 (KPC FM), Saudi Arabia after W12 — leaving only US crude at 294 kbd, barely enough to run one of Taiwan’s two major refineries.

With 150 days of oil reserves and pre-war ME crude dependence of ~70%, Taiwan is drawing down strategic stocks at an alarming rate. The Atlantic Council warns that an extended Hormuz closure could force power rationing by summer, potentially disrupting TSMC semiconductor production with cascading global supply chain impacts.

CPC and Formosa Plastics are scrambling for West African and US spot cargoes, but the 30+ day shipping time from the US Gulf Coast vs. 15 days from the Persian Gulf constrains how quickly alternative supply can scale.
taiwan_lng: Qatar (26 ktons/d in 9–15 Mar — the last pre-FM cargo) disappeared entirely from W12 onward, but Taiwan successfully diversified: Australia surged from 22 to 41 ktons/d to become the dominant supplier, supplemented by Oman (10 ktons/d), Brunei, Equatorial Guinea, Canada, and US spot cargoes.

Gulf LNG share fell from 38% (W11) to 14% (23–29 Mar, Oman only). However, Taiwan’s 11-day gas storage — the lowest among major East Asian LNG importers — leaves virtually no buffer for supply hiccups. Any delay in a single Australian or US cargo could trigger emergency power curtailments.

The diversification is working for now at ~60–72 ktons/d, but Qatar previously supplied ~33% of Taiwan’s LNG; permanently replacing this with spot cargoes at current Asian premiums ($25+/MMBtu) will significantly increase Taiwan Power Company’s fuel costs.
taiwan_gasoil_diesel: Diesel imports fell sharply from 97 kbd (9–15 Mar) to 30 kbd (23–29 Mar) as South Korea, China, and Russia rotated as suppliers. The decline reflects both tightening regional supply and Taiwan’s own diesel rationing measures, with priority given to agriculture and essential transport.
Russia’s brief appearance (4 kbd in 16–22 Mar) signals Taipei’s willingness to accept non-traditional suppliers under crisis conditions, despite political sensitivities.
taiwan_gasoline: Near-zero gasoline imports throughout March — only a single Japanese cargo (25 kbd in 16–22 Mar). Taiwan’s CPC and Formosa refineries still produce sufficient gasoline domestically, but this depends on sustaining crude throughput. If crude imports remain at the current ~294 kbd (vs. ~864 kbd pre-disruption), domestic gasoline production will contract and import needs will spike.
taiwan_naphtha: Total naphtha supply collapse: from 147 kbd (100% Gulf — UAE 128 kbd + Iraq 20 kbd in 9–15 Mar) to absolute zero by 23–29 Mar. Iraq disappeared after the FM on all foreign-operated oilfields; UAE naphtha was cut as ADNOC prioritised crude-over-products for Fujairah loadings.

This is devastating for Formosa Plastics (6th-largest petrochemical company globally) and Taiwan’s downstream chemical sector, which depends heavily on Gulf naphtha feedstock. With South Korea also under naphtha export bans (enforced Mar 27) and China restricting refined exports, Taiwan has almost no regional alternatives — only distant US or European cargoes at extreme premiums.
